docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• नमस्ते।
  • हेलो।
  • हाय।
  • अरे, नमस्ते!
  • अरे, हाय!

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Register Notes Hi! Casual The most common everyday greeting. Hello! Neutral Appropriate almost everywhere. Hey! Casual Friendly and informal. Good morning! Neutral Morning greeting. Good afternoon! Neutral Afternoon greeting. Good evening! Neutral Evening greeting.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text:
• "Hi!" is the safest everyday greeting.
  • "Hello!" is slightly more neutral.
  • "Hey!" is informal.
  • Time-based greetings are common in workplaces and customer-facing situations.

docs.title: 🏠 Home
docs.text:
Hindi: अरे, हाय! खाना तैयार है।

Natural English: Hi! Dinner's ready.

docs.title: 💼 Office
docs.text:
Hindi: सुप्रभात सभी को।

Natural English: Good morning, everyone.

docs.title: 👥 Friends
docs.text:
Hindi: अरे! बहुत दिनों बाद!

Natural English: Hey! Long time no see!

docs.title: 📱 Phone
docs.text:
Hindi: हेलो, क्या मैं राहुल से बात कर सकता हूँ?

Natural English: Hello! May I speak to Rahul?

docs.title: 🛍️ Shop
docs.text:
Hindi: नमस्ते! मैं आपकी कैसे मदद कर सकता हूँ?

Natural English: Hello! How can I help you?

docs.title: ❌ Mistake
docs.text: Hello, myself Rahul.

docs.title: ✅ Natural English
docs.text: Hi! I'm Rahul.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text: Native speakers introduce themselves with "I'm...", not "Myself...".

docs.title: Similar English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Difference Hi there! Slightly warmer. Hey there! More informal. Morning! Casual version of "Good morning."

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: When in doubt, say Hi!

docs.title: Hindi → English
docs.text: अरे, हाय! बहुत दिनों बाद मिले।

docs.title: English → Hindi
docs.text: Good morning! Nice to see you.

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text:
Most common: Hi!

Most formal: Good morning!

Avoid: Hello, myself Rahul.

Remember: English greetings usually continue with small talk.

docs.title: Pattern Family 2 — Asking How Someone Is
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• कैसे हो?
  • कैसी हो?
  • कैसे हैं?
  • क्या हाल है?
  • क्या हाल-चाल है?

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Register Notes How are you? Neutral Universal greeting. How's it going? Casual Very common. How've you been? Casual After a long gap. How are things? Neutral Broader than personal well-being. What's up? Casual Informal greeting.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: These expressions are usually greetings, not genuine requests for detailed updates. A short answer is expected.

docs.title: 👥 Friends
docs.text:
Hindi: अरे! कैसे हो?

Natural English: Hey! How's it going?

docs.title: 💼 Office
docs.text:
Hindi: गुड मॉर्निंग! कैसे हैं?

Natural English: Good morning! How are you?

docs.title: 📱 Phone
docs.text:
Hindi: हेलो! बहुत दिनों बाद। कैसे हो?

Natural English: Hi! How've you been?

docs.title: 🏠 Home
docs.text:
Hindi: सब कैसा चल रहा है?

Natural English: How are things?

docs.title: ☕ Café
docs.text:
Hindi: क्या हाल है?

Natural English: What's up?

docs.title: ❌ Mistake
docs.text: What is your condition?

docs.title: ✅ Natural English
docs.text: How are you?

docs.title: Why?
docs.text: "Condition" refers to health or a situation, not a normal greeting.

docs.title: Similar English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Difference Everything okay? Checks if something is wrong. You okay? More personal. What's new? Asks for recent updates.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Treat "How are you?" as a greeting, not an interview.

docs.title: Hindi → English
docs.text: कैसे हो? बहुत दिनों बाद मिले।

docs.title: English → Hindi
docs.text: How's it going?

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text:
Most common: How are you?

Most casual: What's up?

Avoid: What is your condition?

Remember: Keep your reply short.

docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• मैं ठीक हूँ।
  • बढ़िया हूँ।
  • सब बढ़िया।
  • ठीक-ठाक हूँ।
  • मज़े में हूँ।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Register Notes I'm good. Casual Most common reply. I'm doing well. Neutral Slightly more formal. Pretty good. Casual Positive. Not bad. Casual Means fairly good. I'm doing great. Casual Very positive.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: English speakers usually keep responses short unless invited to elaborate.

docs.title: 👥 Friends
docs.text:
Hindi: मैं बढ़िया हूँ। तुम बताओ?

Natural English: I'm good. How about you?

docs.title: 💼 Office
docs.text:
Hindi: मैं ठीक हूँ, धन्यवाद।

Natural English: I'm doing well, thanks.

docs.title: 📱 Phone
docs.text:
Hindi: सब बढ़िया है।

Natural English: Pretty good.

docs.title: 🏠 Home
docs.text:
Hindi: मैं ठीक-ठाक हूँ।

Natural English: Not bad.

docs.title: ☕ Café
docs.text:
Hindi: मैं मज़े में हूँ।

Natural English: I'm doing great.

docs.title: ❌ Mistake
docs.text: I am fine only.

docs.title: ✅ Natural English
docs.text: I'm good.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text: "Only" is unnecessary and sounds unnatural here.

docs.title: Similar English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Difference Doing alright. Neutral. So far, so good. Things are going well. Couldn't be better. Extremely positive.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Default to I'm good.

docs.title: Hindi → English
docs.text: मैं ठीक हूँ, धन्यवाद।

docs.title: English → Hindi
docs.text: Pretty good, thanks.

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text:
Most common: I'm good.

Avoid: I am fine only.

Remember: Keep it short.

docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• धन्यवाद।
  • शुक्रिया।
  • बहुत-बहुत धन्यवाद।
  • आपका बहुत धन्यवाद।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Register Notes Thanks. Casual Everyday choice. Thank you. Neutral Universal. Thanks a lot. Casual Stronger appreciation. Thank you so much. Neutral Warm gratitude. I really appreciate it. Neutral Genuine appreciation.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: English speakers say "thanks" much more frequently than Hindi speakers, even for small favors.

docs.title: 🛍️ Shop
docs.text:
Hindi: धन्यवाद!

Natural English: Thanks!

docs.title: 💼 Office
docs.text:
Hindi: रिपोर्ट भेजने के लिए धन्यवाद।

Natural English: Thank you for sending the report.

docs.title: 👥 Friends
docs.text:
Hindi: मदद के लिए बहुत-बहुत धन्यवाद।

Natural English: Thanks a lot for helping me.

docs.title: 🍽️ Restaurant
docs.text:
Hindi: खाना बहुत अच्छा था, धन्यवाद।

Natural English: Thank you. The food was great.

docs.title: 📞 Customer Support
docs.text:
Hindi: आपकी मदद के लिए धन्यवाद।

Natural English: I really appreciate your help.

docs.title: ❌ Mistake
docs.text: Many many thanks.

docs.title: ✅ Natural English
docs.text: Thanks a lot.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text: Native speakers don't normally repeat "many".

docs.title: Similar English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Difference Much appreciated. Professional. Thanks again. Repeated gratitude. I owe you one. Informal; implies returning the favor.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: If you're unsure, simply say Thanks!

docs.title: Hindi → English
docs.text: बहुत-बहुत धन्यवाद।

docs.title: English → Hindi
docs.text: I really appreciate it.

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text:
Most common: Thanks.

Most formal: Thank you.

Avoid: Many many thanks.

Remember: Thank people often in English.

docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• कोई बात नहीं।
  • कोई दिक्कत नहीं।
  • अरे, इसकी क्या बात है।
  • कभी भी।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Register Notes You're welcome. Neutral Standard response. No problem. Casual Very common. No worries. Casual Relaxed and friendly. Anytime. Casual Happy to help again. My pleasure. Formal Polite and professional.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: English has fixed responses after someone says "Thank you." Choose the one that matches the situation.

docs.title: 💼 Office
docs.text:
Hindi: धन्यवाद!

Natural English: You're welcome.

docs.title: 👥 Friends
docs.text:
Hindi: थैंक्स यार!

Natural English: No problem.

docs.title: 🏠 Home
docs.text:
Hindi: शुक्रिया!

Natural English: No worries.

docs.title: 🍽️ Restaurant
docs.text:
Hindi: धन्यवाद।

Natural English: My pleasure.

docs.title: 🚗 Friends
docs.text:
Hindi: लिफ्ट देने के लिए धन्यवाद।

Natural English: Anytime.

docs.title: ❌ Mistake
docs.text: There is no problem.

docs.title: ✅ Natural English
docs.text: No problem.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text: Native speakers use the fixed expression "No problem."

docs.title: Similar English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Difference Happy to help. Friendly. Don't mention it. Polite. It was nothing. Downplays the favor.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: After "Thank you", your safest reply is You're welcome.

docs.title: Hindi → English
docs.text: कोई बात नहीं।

docs.title: English → Hindi
docs.text: You're welcome.

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text:
Most common: You're welcome.

Most casual: No problem.

Avoid: There is no problem.

docs.title: Pattern Family 6 — Meeting After a Long Time
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• बहुत दिनों बाद!
  • कितने दिनों बाद मिले!
  • कहाँ थे इतने दिन?
  • बहुत समय बाद मिले।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Register Notes Long time no see! Casual Most common. It's been a while! Casual Warm and natural. It's been ages! Casual Exaggerates the gap. Good to see you again! Neutral Friendly. Where have you been? Casual Usually playful.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: These expressions emphasize the happiness of seeing someone again rather than the exact time that has passed.

docs.title: 👥 Friends
docs.text:
Hindi: अरे! बहुत दिनों बाद!

Natural English: Long time no see!

docs.title: 💼 Office
docs.text:
Hindi: आपको फिर से देखकर अच्छा लगा।

Natural English: Good to see you again.

docs.title: 🏫 Reunion
docs.text:
Hindi: कितने समय बाद मिले!

Natural English: It's been ages!

docs.title: 🏠 Neighbour
docs.text:
Hindi: कहाँ थे इतने दिन?

Natural English: Where have you been?

docs.title: 📱 Phone
docs.text:
Hindi: बहुत समय बाद बात हो रही है।

Natural English: It's been a while!

docs.title: ❌ Mistake
docs.text: We are meeting after many days.

docs.title: ✅ Natural English
docs.text: Long time no see!

docs.title: Why?
docs.text: English uses idiomatic expressions instead of describing the time gap literally.

docs.title: Similar English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Difference Nice seeing you again. Leaving after meeting. Great to see you. General reunion. Welcome back. Used after someone's return.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Celebrate the reunion instead of counting the days.

docs.title: Hindi → English
docs.text: बहुत दिनों बाद मिले।

docs.title: English → Hindi
docs.text: It's been a while!

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text:
Most common: Long time no see!

Most natural: It's been a while!

Avoid: We are meeting after many days.

docs.title: Pattern Family 7 — What Brings You Here?
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• कैसे आना हुआ?
  • यहाँ कैसे आना हुआ?
  • इधर कैसे?
  • आज इधर कैसे?

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Register Notes What brings you here? Neutral Best all-purpose choice. How come you're here? Casual Friendly surprise. What are you doing here? Casual Tone matters. Fancy seeing you here! Casual Expresses pleasant surprise. Didn't expect to see you here! Casual Natural reaction.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: These expressions express surprise at unexpectedly meeting someone. They usually start a friendly conversation.

docs.title: 🛍️ Mall
docs.text:
Hindi: अरे! यहाँ कैसे आना हुआ?

Natural English: Hey! What brings you here?

docs.title: ☕ Café
docs.text:
Hindi: तुम यहाँ?

Natural English: Fancy seeing you here!

docs.title: ✈️ Airport
docs.text:
Hindi: आज इधर कैसे?

Natural English: Didn't expect to see you here!

docs.title: 💼 Office Lobby
docs.text:
Hindi: कैसे आना हुआ?

Natural English: What brings you here today?

docs.title: 🏥 Hospital
docs.text:
Hindi: यहाँ कैसे?

Natural English: How come you're here?

docs.title: ❌ Mistake
docs.text: How did you come here?

docs.title: ✅ Natural English
docs.text: What brings you here?

docs.title: Why?
docs.text: "How did you come?" asks about transportation, not the reason for visiting.

docs.title: Similar English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Difference Small world! Coincidental meeting. Nice surprise! Expresses pleasant surprise.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Think why, not how.

docs.title: Hindi → English
docs.text: अरे! यहाँ कैसे आना हुआ?

docs.title: English → Hindi
docs.text: What brings you here?

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text:
Most common: What brings you here?

Avoid: How did you come here?

docs.title: Pattern Family 8 — Where Have You Been?
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• कहाँ गायब थे?
  • कहाँ थे इतने दिन?
  • दिख ही नहीं रहे थे।
  • नज़र ही नहीं आ रहे थे।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Register Notes Where have you been? Casual Most common. Haven't seen you in a while. Neutral Warm and friendly. You've disappeared! Casual Playful exaggeration. I was wondering where you were. Neutral Gentle. Good to see you again. Neutral Positive reunion.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: These expressions communicate that someone has been absent and that you've noticed it.

docs.title: 👥 Friends
docs.text:
Hindi: कहाँ गायब थे?

Natural English: Where have you been?

docs.title: 💼 Office
docs.text:
Hindi: काफी दिनों से नहीं दिखे।

Natural English: Haven't seen you in a while.

docs.title: 🏋️ Gym
docs.text:
Hindi: आजकल दिखते ही नहीं।

Natural English: You've disappeared!

docs.title: 🏠 Neighbour
docs.text:
Hindi: सोच रहा था कहाँ थे।

Natural English: I was wondering where you were.

docs.title: 📱 Chat
docs.text:
Hindi: बहुत दिनों बाद ऑनलाइन दिखे।

Natural English: Good to see you again.

docs.title: ❌ Mistake
docs.text: You were not visible.

docs.title: ✅ Natural English
docs.text: Haven't seen you in a while.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text: English focuses on not seeing the person, not them being "visible."

docs.title: Similar English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Difference Long time no see. Greeting after meeting. Missed seeing you. More emotional.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Say Haven't seen you..., not You were not visible.

docs.title: Hindi → English
docs.text: कहाँ गायब थे इतने दिन?

docs.title: English → Hindi
docs.text: Haven't seen you in a while.

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text:
Most common: Where have you been?

Most natural: Haven't seen you in a while.

Avoid: You were not visible.

docs.title: Pattern Family 9 — Nice to Meet You
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• आपसे मिलकर खुशी हुई।
  • आपसे मिलकर अच्छा लगा।
  • मिलकर अच्छा लगा।
  • अच्छा लगा मिलकर।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Register Notes Nice to meet you. Neutral First meeting. Nice meeting you. Neutral Usually when leaving. It was great meeting you. Casual Warm farewell. It was a pleasure meeting you. Formal Professional. Great to see you. Casual For someone you've met before.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Use Nice to meet you during introductions. Use Nice meeting you when ending the conversation. Great to see you is for people you already know.

docs.title: 💼 Interview
docs.text:
Hindi: आपसे मिलकर खुशी हुई।

Natural English: It was a pleasure meeting you.

docs.title: 🤝 Networking Event
docs.text:
Hindi: आपसे मिलकर अच्छा लगा।

Natural English: Nice to meet you.

docs.title: 👥 Friends
docs.text:
Hindi: फिर से मिलकर अच्छा लगा।

Natural English: Great to see you again.

docs.title: 🏠 New Neighbour
docs.text:
Hindi: मैं आपका नया पड़ोसी हूँ। मिलकर अच्छा लगा।

Natural English: I'm your new neighbour. Nice to meet you.

docs.title: 📞 Business Meeting
docs.text:
Hindi: आज आपसे मिलकर अच्छा लगा।

Natural English: It was great meeting you today.

docs.title: ❌ Mistake
docs.text: I am happy after meeting you.

docs.title: ✅ Natural English
docs.text: Nice meeting you.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text: English uses established expressions rather than describing your feelings literally.

docs.title: Similar English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Difference Glad we met. Informal. Lovely meeting you. Warmer. Great seeing you again. Reunion.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Meet = first meeting. See = someone you already know.

docs.title: Hindi → English
docs.text: आपसे मिलकर बहुत अच्छा लगा।

docs.title: English → Hindi
docs.text: It was a pleasure meeting you.

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text:
First meeting: Nice to meet you.

Leaving: Nice meeting you.

Avoid: I am happy after meeting you.

docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• फिर मिलेंगे।
  • संपर्क में रहना।
  • मिलते रहना।
  • बात करते रहेंगे।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Register Notes Keep in touch. Neutral Most common. See you around. Casual Expect to meet again. Talk to you soon. Neutral Phone or chat. Let's stay in touch. Neutral Friendly. Hope to see you again soon. Neutral Warm farewell.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: These expressions end a conversation positively without promising a specific future meeting.

docs.title: 💼 Office
docs.text:
Hindi: संपर्क में रहना।

Natural English: Keep in touch.

docs.title: 👥 Friends
docs.text:
Hindi: फिर मिलेंगे!

Natural English: See you around!

docs.title: 📱 Phone
docs.text:
Hindi: ठीक है, फिर बात करेंगे।

Natural English: Talk to you soon.

docs.title: 🎓 Conference
docs.text:
Hindi: संपर्क में रहेंगे।

Natural English: Let's stay in touch.

docs.title: 🏠 Neighbour
docs.text:
Hindi: फिर मिलेंगे।

Natural English: Hope to see you again soon.

docs.title: ❌ Mistake
docs.text: We will meet again and again.

docs.title: ✅ Natural English
docs.text: Keep in touch.

docs.title: Why?
docs.text: Native speakers usually express staying connected rather than repeatedly meeting.

docs.title: Similar English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Difference Catch you later. Informal. Speak soon. Phone/chat. Until next time. Warm farewell.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Think stay connected, not keep meeting.

docs.title: Hindi → English
docs.text: संपर्क में रहना।

docs.title: English → Hindi
docs.text: Let's stay in touch.

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text:
Most common: Keep in touch.

Phone: Talk to you soon.

Avoid: We will meet again and again.

docs.title: Pattern Family 11 — Starting a Conversation Naturally
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• और बताओ।
  • और सुनाओ।
  • क्या चल रहा है?
  • क्या नया?
  • और क्या हाल?

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Register Notes What's been going on? Casual Great after not talking for a while. What's new? Casual Asks for recent updates. How have things been? Neutral Friendly and natural. What's been keeping you busy? Neutral Focuses on recent activities. So, what's going on? Casual Good conversation opener.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: These expressions invite conversation rather than asking for a detailed report.

docs.title: 👥 Friends
docs.text:
Hindi: और बताओ, क्या चल रहा है?

Natural English: So, what's been going on?

docs.title: 💼 Office
docs.text:
Hindi: और, प्रोजेक्ट कैसा चल रहा है?

Natural English: So, how have things been with the project?

docs.title: ☕ Café
docs.text:
Hindi: क्या नया?

Natural English: What's new?

docs.title: 📱 Phone
docs.text:
Hindi: और सुनाओ, सब ठीक?

Natural English: What's been going on? Everything good?

docs.title: 🏠 Family
docs.text:
Hindi: और क्या हाल?

Natural English: How have things been?

docs.title: ❌ Mistake
docs.text: Tell me more.

docs.title: ✅ Natural English
docs.text: What's been going on?

docs.title: Why?
docs.text: "Tell me more" is used after someone has already started speaking, not as a greeting.

docs.title: Similar English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Difference How's everything? General well-being. Anything new? Recent updates. How's life? Broader and informal.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Think of these as conversation openers.

docs.title: Hindi → English
docs.text: और बताओ, आजकल क्या चल रहा है?

docs.title: English → Hindi
docs.text: What's new?

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text:
Most common: What's new?

Best opener: What's been going on?

Avoid: Tell me more.

docs.title: Pattern Family 12 — Asking About Someone's Availability
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• फ्री हो?
  • बिज़ी तो नहीं हो?
  • दो मिनट हैं?
  • टाइम है?
  • अभी बात कर सकते हो?

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text: Expression Register Do you have a minute? Neutral Got a minute? Casual Are you busy right now? Neutral Is now a good time? Neutral Can we talk for a minute? Neutral

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: These expressions politely check whether someone is available before starting a conversation.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Do you have a minute?
  • Got a minute?
  • Is now a good time to talk?
  • Are you busy right now?
  • Can we talk for a minute?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Are you free?

✅ Do you have a minute?

Why? Native speakers often ask about time rather than "freedom."

docs.title: Similar English Expressions
docs.text:
• Do you have a few minutes?
  • Mind if I ask you something?

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Ask for a minute, not whether someone is free.

docs.title: Mini Practice
docs.text:
Hindi → English: बिज़ी तो नहीं हो?

English → Hindi: Is now a good time?

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text: Most common: Do you have a minute?

docs.title: Pattern Family 13 — Excusing Yourself Before Speaking
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• एक बात पूछनी थी।
  • एक बात कहनी थी।
  • अगर बुरा न मानो...
  • परेशान तो नहीं कर रहा?

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Can I ask you something?
  • I wanted to ask you something.
  • Do you have a quick second?
  • I hope I'm not bothering you.
  • Can I run something by you?

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Used to soften the start of a conversation or request.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Can I ask you something?
  • I hope I'm not bothering you.
  • Do you have a quick second?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ One thing I wanted to tell.

✅ I wanted to tell you something.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Soften the opening before making your request.

docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• सुनो...
  • ज़रा सुनना...
  • अरे सुनो...
  • एक मिनट...

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Excuse me.
  • Hey.
  • Listen.
  • Can I get your attention for a second?
  • One second.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Choose expressions based on familiarity and formality.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Excuse me, could you help me?
  • Hey, listen.
  • One second, please.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Listen me.

✅ Listen.

✅ Listen to me.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: It's "listen to someone", not "listen someone".

docs.title: Pattern Family 15 — Checking If Someone Can Hear You
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• मेरी आवाज़ आ रही है?
  • सुन पा रहे हो?
  • हेलो, सुन रहे हो?
  • आवाज़ कट रही है?

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Can you hear me?
  • Can you hear me clearly?
  • Are you able to hear me?
  • Is my voice breaking up?
  • Can you hear me okay?

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Used mainly during phone or video calls to confirm audio quality.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Can you hear me?
  • Can you hear me clearly?
  • Is my voice breaking up?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Is my voice coming?

✅ Can you hear me?

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Ask what the listener can hear, not what your voice is doing.

docs.title: Pattern Family 16 — Ending a Conversation Politely
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• अच्छा, चलता हूँ।
  • ठीक है, फिर बात करते हैं।
  • अब निकलता हूँ।
  • चलता हूँ फिर।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• I'd better get going.
  • I'll let you go.
  • Talk to you later.
  • See you later.
  • I've got to run.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Native speakers often soften departures instead of saying "I'm leaving."

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• I'd better get going.
  • I'll let you get back to work.
  • Talk to you later.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ I am going.

✅ I'd better get going.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: End naturally instead of announcing you're leaving.

docs.title: Pattern Family 17 — Asking Someone to Wait
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• एक मिनट।
  • ज़रा रुको।
  • थोड़ी देर रुको।
  • बस दो मिनट।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Just a second.
  • Hang on.
  • Hold on.
  • Give me a minute.
  • One moment, please.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Choose expressions based on formality. "Hang on" is casual; "One moment, please" is more formal.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Hang on, I'm coming.
  • Just a second.
  • Give me a minute.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Wait.

✅ Just a second.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Native speakers often soften "wait" with "just" or "one moment."

docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• एक मिनट...
  • बीच में बोल रहा हूँ...
  • माफ़ कीजिए, लेकिन...
  • बीच में टोक रहा हूँ...

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Sorry to interrupt.
  • Excuse me.
  • If I may...
  • Can I jump in for a second?
  • May I add something?

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Use these expressions to enter a conversation politely without sounding rude.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Sorry to interrupt, but I have a quick question.
  • Excuse me, may I add something?
  • Can I jump in for a second?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ I am cutting your talk.

✅ Sorry to interrupt.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Apologize briefly before interrupting.

docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• तो मैं कह रहा था...
  • जहाँ थे वहीं से...
  • हाँ, तो...
  • वैसे...

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Anyway...
  • As I was saying...
  • So, where were we?
  • Getting back to...
  • To continue...

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: These help you return to the original topic after an interruption.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Anyway, as I was saying...
  • So, where were we?
  • Getting back to the report...

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ As I was telling.

✅ As I was saying.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: "As I was saying..." is the classic way to resume.

docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• समझ गया।
  • ठीक है।
  • अच्छा।
  • समझ में आ गया।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Got it.
  • I understand.
  • That makes sense.
  • Okay, I see.
  • Understood.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Choose the expression based on formality. "Got it" is conversational; "Understood" is more formal.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Got it. I'll do that.
  • Okay, I see.
  • That makes sense.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ I understood.

✅ I understand. / Got it.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Use "Got it." for everyday conversations.

docs.title: Pattern Family 21 — Asking Someone to Repeat
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• फिर से बोलो।
  • दोबारा कहना।
  • एक बार और बोलोगे?
  • क्या कहा?

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Could you say that again?
  • Sorry, could you repeat that?
  • I didn't catch that.
  • Could you say that one more time?
  • Sorry?

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Use polite forms in professional settings. "Sorry?" is common in casual conversations.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Sorry, could you repeat that?
  • I didn't catch that.
  • Could you say that again?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Repeat again.

✅ Could you repeat that?

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Ask to repeat that, not "again" after repeat.

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text: Most common: See Natural English Expressions above.

docs.title: Pattern Family 22 — Checking If You Heard Correctly
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• मैंने सही सुना?
  • मतलब...?
  • तुम कह रहे हो कि...?
  • क्या तुमने कहा...?

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Did I hear that right?
  • So, you're saying...?
  • Did you say...?
  • Just to make sure...

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Use these to confirm important information before responding.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Did I hear that right?
  • So, you're saying the meeting is tomorrow?
  • Just to make sure...

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ I heard correct?

✅ Did I hear that right?

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Confirm before assuming.

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text: Most common: See Natural English Expressions above.

docs.title: Pattern Family 23 — Asking Someone to Speak More Slowly
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• थोड़ा धीरे बोलिए।
  • ज़रा धीरे बोलो।
  • बहुत तेज़ बोल रहे हो।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Could you speak a little more slowly?
  • Would you mind slowing down?
  • You're speaking a bit fast.
  • Could you slow down a little?

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Be polite when asking someone to slow down.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Could you slow down a little?
  • You're speaking a bit fast.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Speak slow.

✅ Speak more slowly.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Prefer "slow down" in conversation.

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text: Most common: See Natural English Expressions above.

docs.title: Pattern Family 24 — Asking Someone to Speak Louder
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• ज़रा ज़ोर से बोलो।
  • आवाज़ नहीं आ रही।
  • थोड़ा ऊँचा बोलिए।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Could you speak up?
  • I can't hear you.
  • Could you speak a little louder?
  • Your voice is very soft.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: "Speak up" is the most natural everyday expression.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Could you speak up?
  • I can't hear you.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Speak loudly.

✅ Could you speak up?

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: "Speak up" is the idiomatic choice.

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text: Most common: See Natural English Expressions above.

docs.title: Pattern Family 25 — Confirming Everything Is Clear
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• सब क्लियर?
  • समझ आया?
  • सब ठीक?
  • कोई सवाल?

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Is everything clear?
  • Does that make sense?
  • Any questions?
  • Are we on the same page?

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Common in meetings, teaching and explanations.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Does that make sense?
  • Any questions before we continue?
  • Are we on the same page?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Everything clear?

✅ Is everything clear?

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Use a complete question in formal situations.

docs.title: Quick Recap
docs.text: Most common: See Natural English Expressions above.

docs.title: Pattern Family 26 — Asking for a Moment
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• एक सेकंड।
  • बस एक मिनट।
  • थोड़ा समय दो।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Just a moment.
  • Give me a second.
  • One moment, please.
  • Bear with me.
  • Give me a minute.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Use these when you need a brief pause before continuing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Just a moment.
  • Give me a second.
  • One moment, please.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Wait.

✅ Just a moment.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Use 'just' to soften the request.

docs.title: Pattern Family 27 — Checking If Someone Is Still There
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• हेलो?
  • सुन रहे हो?
  • लाइन पर हो?

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Are you still there?
  • Can you still hear me?
  • Hello?
  • Are you with me?

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Useful during phone and video calls.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Are you still there?
  • Can you still hear me?
  • Hello?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Are you there only?

✅ Are you still there?

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Ask about presence, not 'only'.

docs.title: Pattern Family 28 — Asking for a Quick Favor
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• एक काम करोगे?
  • एक मदद चाहिए।
  • एक छोटा सा काम था।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Could you do me a favor?
  • Can I ask a quick favor?
  • Could you help me with something?
  • Would you mind helping me?

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Common before making a small request.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Could you do me a favor?
  • Can I ask a quick favor?
  • Could you help me with something?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Do one work.

✅ Could you do me a favor?

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: 'Favor' is the natural word.

docs.title: Pattern Family 29 — Checking If Someone Is Following
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• समझ आ रहा है?
  • मेरी बात समझ रहे हो?
  • फॉलो कर रहे हो?

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Are you following?
  • Does that make sense?
  • Are you with me?
  • Following so far?

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Often used while explaining something.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Are you following?
  • Does that make sense?
  • Are you with me?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Are you understanding?

✅ Are you following?

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Prefer 'following' while explaining.

docs.title: Pattern Family 30 — Inviting Someone to Continue
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• आगे बताओ।
  • फिर?
  • उसके बाद?

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Go on.
  • Please continue.
  • And then?
  • What happened next?
  • I'm listening.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Encourages the speaker to continue.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Go on.
  • Please continue.
  • And then?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Tell further.

✅ Go on.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: 'Go on' is the natural prompt.

docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• हम्म...
  • सोचने दो...
  • एक मिनट सोचूँ...

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Let me think.
  • Hmm...
  • Give me a second to think.
  • That's a good question.
  • Let me see.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Useful when you need a moment before answering.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Let me think.
  • Hmm...
  • Give me a second to think.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Wait, I am thinking.

✅ Let me think.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: "Let me think" is the standard phrase.

docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• अच्छा?
  • सच में?
  • फिर?

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Really?
  • Is that so?
  • No way!
  • Tell me more.
  • What happened next?

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Shows curiosity and keeps the conversation going.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Really?
  • Is that so?
  • No way!

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Tell further.

✅ Tell me more.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Ask naturally to encourage the speaker.

docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• अरे वाह!
  • सच में!
  • क्या बात है!

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Wow!
  • Really?
  • That's amazing!
  • No way!
  • You've got to be kidding!

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Different expressions fit different levels of surprise.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Wow!
  • Really?
  • That's amazing!

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ What a thing!

✅ Wow!

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Use short reactions.

docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• हाँ सही है।
  • बिल्कुल।
  • ठीक है।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Exactly.
  • Absolutely.
  • I agree.
  • Sounds good.
  • You're right.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Common responses in everyday conversations.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• Exactly.
  • Absolutely.
  • I agree.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ I am agree.

✅ I agree.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Never use 'I am agree'.

docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• हूँ।
  • अच्छा।
  • समझ गया।
  • हम्म।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• I see.
  • Uh-huh.
  • Right.
  • Got it.
  • I understand.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: These listener responses keep the conversation flowing.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• I see.
  • Uh-huh.
  • Right.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ Yes only.

✅ I see.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Small acknowledgements sound natural.

docs.title: Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• ऐसा नहीं है।
  • मुझे नहीं लगता।
  • मैं सहमत नहीं हूँ।

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• I'm not so sure about that.
  • I don't think so.
  • I see it differently.
  • I'm afraid I disagree.
  • Not necessarily.

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Disagree without sounding confrontational.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• I'm not so sure about that.
  • I don't think so.
  • I see it differently.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ You are wrong.

✅ I'm not so sure about that.

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Disagree with the idea, not the person.

docs.title: Pattern Family 37 — Asking for Someone's Opinion
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• तुम्हारा क्या कहना है?
  • तुम्हें क्या लगता है?
  • क्या राय है?

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• What do you think?
  • What's your opinion?
  • How do you see it?
  • What's your take?
  • Any thoughts?

docs.title: Nuance
docs.text: Invite the other person to share their view.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• What do you think?
  • What's your opinion?
  • How do you see it?

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
❌ What's in your mind?

✅ What do you think?

docs.title: Memory Tip
docs.text: Use fixed opinion phrases.

docs.title: Pattern Family 38 — Buying Time in Conversation
Hindi Pattern
docs.text:
• उम्म...
  • मतलब...
  • देखो...

docs.title: Natural English Expressions
docs.text:
• Well...
  • Let me think...
  • You know...
